hook: A British major's twin Mamluk houses, packed with hoarded treasure
facts: Two 16th-17th century houses, Bayt al-Kritliyya (House of the Cretan Woman), knocked together
Restored and furnished by British officer John Gayer-Anderson from 1935 until 1942
Stuffed with his eclectic collection of furniture, art and antiquities across themed rooms
Used as a location in the 1977 Bond film The Spy Who Loved Me
legend: Local legend tied the house to a haunted well and a magical cult, retold in Gayer-Anderson's own folklore notes
w: A British army doctor talked his way into two adjoining Mamluk houses beside Ibn Tulun and filled them with hoarded furniture, carpets and curios from across the empire. Mashrabiya screens, a secret harem balcony, themed rooms left as he kept them. Bond filmed a rooftop here. One man's magpie dream in stone.

hook: An Ottoman merchant's house hidden down a narrow lane off al-Muizz
facts: First section built in 1648, expanded by Sheikh Ahmed as-Suhaymi who bought it in 1796
Built around a green courtyard with mashrabiya screens and separate men's and women's floors
Sold in 1931 to the Committee for the Preservation of Arab Antiquities
Restored in 1996 and now open as a house-museum of old Cairo life
w: Step off al-Muizz down a crooked lane and a blank wall opens onto a courtyard of palms and carved wood. A wealthy sheikh swallowed his neighbours' houses into one rambling mansion, with latticed screens to keep the women unseen and the rooms cool. Marble floors and ceilings survive intact. Old Cairo, behind a door.

hook: A sultan's mausoleum, madrasa and hospital that ran for 500 years
facts: Commissioned by Sultan al-Mansur Qalawun and completed in just 13 months, 1284-85
Built partly with forced labour of passers-by and Mongol prisoners of war
Its bimaristan (hospital) treated patients for over 500 years before demolition in 1910
The mausoleum dome was rebuilt in 1908 after an Ottoman governor pulled it down
w: Thirteen months to build, by gangs of Mongol war-captives and any passer-by the foreman could grab off the street. A Mamluk sultan got his tomb, a law school and a hospital that kept treating the sick for five centuries. The mausoleum's stained light still pours over Qalawun's grave. Mamluk power, set in stone fast.

hook: A fountain-and-school on a triangle where the old high street forks
facts: Built in 1744 by the Janissary leader Abd al-Rahman Katkhuda, who died in 1776
Combines a sabil (public water fountain) below with a kuttab (Quran school) above
Stands on a triangular plot where al-Muizz Street splits into two branches
A landmark of Ottoman-era Cairo blending Mamluk and Ottoman detail
w: Where the medieval high street splits in two, a Janissary commander wedged a little jewel-box onto the triangle: free water on the ground floor for the thirsty, a Quran school above for the young. Tiled, bowed and double-fronted to face both forks. Charity and showing off, in the same building.

hook: A Fatimid gate where heads were displayed, with minarets you can climb
facts: Stone gate rebuilt in 1092, one of three surviving gates of Fatimid Cairo
Twin minarets added 1415-22 by Sultan al-Mu'ayyad atop the bastions, open to climb
Site of public executions and severed-head displays in Mamluk times
The last Mamluk sultan, Tuman Bay II, was hanged here in 1517
legend: Locals long pinned scraps of cloth and teeth to the door as votive offerings, believing the gate could cure ailments
w: Severed heads once decorated this Fatimid gate, and the last Mamluk sultan swung from a rope above the crowd. Climb the twin minarets a later sultan stacked on top and the medieval roofscape unrolls to the Citadel. They say the supplicant sick once nailed teeth and rags to the doors to be healed. The cruel old mouth of the city.

hook: Egypt's oldest intact mosque, with a minaret you climb on the outside
facts: Built 876-879 by Ahmad ibn Tulun, the oldest mosque in Egypt surviving in original form
Its spiral minaret has an external staircase wrapping the outside
The design echoes the helical Malwiya minaret of Abbasid Samarra in Iraq
Vast brick courtyard covers over 26,000 square metres with its outer enclosure
legend: Legend says Ibn Tulun designed the spiral minaret after idly winding a strip of paper round his finger
w: Acres of bare brick and crenellated emptiness, the oldest mosque in Egypt still standing as built in 879. Climb the corkscrew minaret on an outside stair, an idea borrowed from Samarra in Iraq. Legend says Ibn Tulun drew the spiral after twisting a strip of paper round his finger. Severe, ancient, gloriously quiet.

hook: A Coptic church suspended over a Roman fortress gate
facts: Nave built across the southern gate towers of the Roman Babylon Fortress, hence 'hanging'
Among Cairo's oldest churches, first documented in the 9th century
Became the seat of the Coptic Pope around 1047, moved from Alexandria
Holds a glass floor panel revealing the Roman stonework below
legend: Tradition holds the Holy Family sheltered nearby during their flight into Egypt
w: Climb twenty-nine steps to a church that floats: its wooden nave straddles the gate towers of a Roman fort, so the floor literally hangs in the air. A glass panel lets you peer down to the stone below. Seat of the Coptic popes from the 11th century, with an ebony pulpit on thirteen pillars. Faith built over a ruin.

hook: The storeroom that held a thousand years of buried Jewish paper
facts: Synagogue in old Fustat, on a site used since the 9th century
Its geniza (storeroom) preserved roughly 400,000 manuscript fragments
The Cairo Geniza, hauled to Cambridge in 1896-97 by Solomon Schechter, reshaped medieval scholarship
Documents span Hebrew, Aramaic and Judeo-Arabic across centuries of daily life
legend: Tradition marks the spot as where the infant Moses was found, and where the prophet Jeremiah once prayed
w: For a thousand years the community here refused to throw away any paper bearing God's name, so it all went up into a sealed loft. When scholars finally cracked it open in the 1890s, some 400,000 scraps spilled out: shopping lists, love letters, lawsuits. Carted to Cambridge, it rebuilt the medieval world. A bin that became an archive.

hook: A vast amphitheatre church carved into the cliff above garbage city
facts: Largest church in the Middle East by seating, cut into the Mokattam cliff
Sits above the Zabbaleen quarter, Cairo's Coptic garbage-collector community
Built from the 1970s after the Zabbaleen settled the area
Cliff faces carved with vast biblical reliefs by Polish artist Mario
legend: Named for Simon the Tanner, whom legend credits with moving the Mokattam mountain by prayer in the 10th century
w: Climb through the Zabbaleen quarter, where Coptic families sort the city's rubbish by hand, and the lane opens into a cathedral hacked from the cliff seating thousands. Reliefs of scripture cut straight into the rock face. Named for a tanner whom legend says moved this very mountain by prayer. Sacred space above the trash.

hook: A necropolis where the living have moved in among the tombs
facts: Islamic cemetery below the Mokattam Hills, running miles north to south
Tomb enclosures with rooms have long housed families of the living
Some plots have water, power and shops woven through the graves
Centuries old, used continuously since the early Islamic city
w: Miles of domed tombs under the Mokattam ridge, and threaded through them: washing lines, kettles, children, shops. Families have lived inside the grave enclosures for generations, the dead as silent neighbours. Not the morbid scene outsiders imagine but a working quarter. Cairo's strangest address, where the cemetery breathes.

hook: Mamluk sultans' tomb-mosques marooned in a desert graveyard
facts: Royal funerary district laid out by the Mamluk sultans beyond the city walls
Dominated by the domed funerary complex of Sultan Qaitbay (1470s)
Qaitbay's carved stone dome features on Egypt's one-pound note
Tombs sit among the living quarters of the wider City of the Dead
w: Out past the walls, where there was only sand, Mamluk sultans built tomb-mosques grand as palaces to be buried in style. Qaitbay's dome, every inch carved with stone lacework, is so fine it ended up on the banknote. Now goats and washing share the lanes between sultans. Faded majesty among the graves.

hook: A prince's fantasy palace mixing every Islamic style at once
facts: Built 1899-1929 by Prince Mohamed Ali Tewfik, who never reached the throne
Five separate buildings in Fatimid, Mamluk, Ottoman, Andalusian and Persian styles
Set in Persian gardens within an English-style park on the Nile
Includes a private mosque, a throne hall and a hunting-trophy museum
w: A prince who waited his whole life for a throne that never came built this instead: a palace that can't choose a style, lurching from Ottoman to Andalusian to Persian room by room. Stained glass, a silver-clad throne, a room of mounted gazelle heads, rare banyans in the garden. Royal restlessness, frozen on Rhoda island.

hook: An artificial grotto and fish tanks tunnelled into a Khedival hill
facts: Laid out in 1867 under Khedive Ismail as the Gabalaya garden
Built around a man-made rock grotto honeycombed with tunnels
Once held aquarium tanks of Nile and Red Sea fish, plus preserved specimens
A shaded retreat in the heart of Zamalek's island district
w: A Khedive who loved a folly raised an artificial mountain in his Zamalek garden and bored caves through it, lined with tanks of Nile and Red Sea fish. The fish have mostly gone, the dim tunnels and dripping grotto remain, lovers sheltering in the cool. Faded Victorian whimsy on a Nile island. Wonderfully past its prime.

hook: A lotus-shaped tower locals nicknamed Nasser's Pineapple
facts: Concrete tower 187 metres tall, completed in 1961 on Gezira island
Its latticework casing takes the form of a stylised lotus plant
Long story holds it was built to spite the CIA, who tried to bribe Nasser
Nasser reportedly handed the cash to the state and built the tower as a rebuke
legend: Cairenes nicknamed it 'Nasser's Pineapple', a jab at the lattice casing and at the leader
w: Story goes the CIA slid Nasser a few million dollars to soften him up; he took the cash, handed it to the treasury, and spent it on this concrete lotus to taunt them. Cairenes promptly nicknamed it Nasser's Pineapple. From the top the whole jumble of the city spreads to the pyramids. Spite, with a viewing deck.

hook: The mansion whose name sparked a revolution: House of the Nation
facts: Home of nationalist leader Saad Zaghloul (1857-1927), founder of the Wafd Party
His 1919 exile triggered the revolution; crowds marched on this house
Preserved with Art Nouveau dining room, Turkish baths and a 5,000-book library
Saad and his wife are buried in a pharaonic-style mausoleum nearby
w: When the British shipped Saad Zaghloul off to exile in 1919, Cairo erupted and the crowds marched on his house, which they took to calling the House of the Nation. The name stuck. Inside: Art Nouveau dining room, Turkish bath, a library of five thousand books, all left as he kept it. Where modern Egypt found its voice.

hook: Egypt's oldest natural-history collection, with a primate jaw to match
facts: First geological museum in the Arab world, opened to the public in 1904
Moved to the Maadi corniche in 1982 when its downtown home fell to the Metro
Holds fossils, meteorites, and the famous Fayum primate finds
Displays specimens of early primate Aegyptopithecus, key to ape evolution
w: The Arab world's first geology museum, evicted from downtown when the Metro wanted its plot, now parked quietly on the Nile corniche. Inside, meteorites, dinosaur bone, and the small jaw of Aegyptopithecus, a 30-million-year-old creature dug from the Fayum that sits near the root of the ape family tree. Deep time, dusty cases.

hook: The cafe where revolutionaries plotted and Mahfouz held court
facts: Opened 1908 on Talaat Harb Street in the Belle Epoque downtown
1919 revolutionaries met in the basement and printed leaflets on its press
Site of a 1919 assassination attempt on the prime minister
Naguib Mahfouz wrote here; Nasser is said to have plotted the 1952 coup nearby
w: Behind a modest wooden front, a century of conspiracy: 1919 rebels ran a printing press in the cellar and someone took a shot at the prime minister from a table. Naguib Mahfouz wrote here on Fridays with the door locked. The waiters are old, the photos yellowing, the coffee strong. Cairo's intellectuals, brewed dark.

hook: A faded Swiss patisserie that once iced cakes for kings
facts: Founded 1909 by the Swiss Groppi family on Talaat Harb Square
Famed for chocolates, ice cream and a glittering tiled rotunda entrance
Patronised by Cairo's interwar elite and royalty in its heyday
Slid into decades of decline before recent restoration attempts
legend: Cairenes say Groppi chocolates were flown to King Farouk and once to wartime Allied officers across the desert
w: Step through the mosaic archway and you're in the glamour of 1930s Cairo, when this Swiss patisserie iced cakes for kings and packed chocolates that, they say, were flown to officers across the desert war. The gilt has dimmed, the marble cracked, the ice cream soldiers on. Belle Epoque sweetness, beautifully gone to seed.

hook: A contemporary art house wedged between potters and Coptic ruins
facts: Independent art and culture centre founded 2008 in the Fustat potters' quarter
Galleries, performance stages and an outdoor cinema in a converted building
Sits among Roman, Coptic and Islamic remains of Egypt's oldest city
Survived threatened demolition by road schemes in the 2020s
w: Down among the kilns and clay heaps of Fustat, Cairo's oldest ground, an artist carved out galleries, stages and a rooftop cinema for work the official halls won't touch. Wedged between potters' workshops and Coptic ruins, it has dodged the bulldozers more than once. Egypt's restless contemporary scene, on the city's oldest dirt.

hook: A cliff of a mosque so vast it doubled as a fortress
facts: Built 1356-63 for Sultan an-Nasir Hasan, a peak of Mamluk architecture
Towering walls and a vaulted cross-plan of four schools of law
Used as an artillery platform to bombard the Citadel during revolts
Sultan Hasan was murdered before its completion and never buried in its dome
legend: It was rumoured the architect had his hand cut off so he could never build its equal
w: Walls like a sea-cliff, a courtyard where four soaring vaults mark the four schools of law. So massive that rebels later mounted cannon on its roof to shell the Citadel opposite. The sultan who built it was murdered first and never lay in his own grand dome. Legend says they maimed the architect so he'd build no rival. Sheer Mamluk nerve.

hook: A Mamluk mosque lined with imported Ottoman blue tiles
facts: Completed 1347 for the emir Shams ad-Din Aqsunqur
Nicknamed Blue Mosque for blue-and-green Iznik-style tiles added in the 1650s
Tiles installed by Ottoman governor Ibrahim Agha during a restoration
A funerary mosque holding the tombs of its founder and several royal children
w: From outside, a plain Mamluk mosque on a battered backstreet. Inside, a wall of blue: hundreds of Ottoman flower-tiles shipped in by a 17th-century governor who fancied the Iznik look. The founder and a clutch of small royal coffins rest beneath them. Cool, dim, and a little melancholy. The bluest corner of Islamic Cairo.

hook: A garden built on five centuries of rubbish, hiding a lost wall
facts: Opened 2005 on the Darassa mound, the city's main rubbish tip for 500 years
Funded by the Aga Khan, who cleared some 80,000 truckloads of debris
Digging uncovered a buried 12th-century Ayyubid city wall begun by Salah al-Din
Three city water reservoirs sit hidden beneath the lawns
w: For five hundred years Cairo tipped its rubbish here until the mound stood higher than the minarets. The Aga Khan hauled away eighty thousand truckloads, laid out formal gardens, and in the digging found a thing nobody knew was there: a lost Ayyubid wall begun by Salah al-Din, swallowed by the trash. A park resurrected from the dump.
